column1: # These are CI-specific suppressions for the HTTP demo-mode smoke container.
# In production, Traefik terminates TLS and adds HSTS; cookies carry the Secure
# flag via SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE. None of these apply over plain HTTP in CI.

column3: Cookie without Secure flag — expected over HTTP in CI; Traefik adds TLS in production
X-XSS-Protection header — obsolete header intentionally not set (superseded by CSP)
Strict-Transport-Security — no TLS in CI demo; Traefik sets HSTS in production
Timestamp Disclosure — HTML timestamps are informational, not sensitive
HTTP Only Site — same as 10011/10035: CI smoke container is plain HTTP by design; Traefik terminates TLS in production

column1: # False positives confirmed after first ZAP run (2026-05-31)

column3: Anti-CSRF: ZAP passive scanner does not execute JS and therefore misses our meta-tag + submit-event CSRF injection (Flask-WTF CSRFProtect is active)
Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy require-corp: cannot be set — Leaflet map tiles (basemaps.cartocdn.com) are external images that do not send CORP headers and would be blocked
Suspicious Comments: ZAP flagged a JSDoc comment in ui.js ("auto-submits") — not sensitive
Cache-Control informational notices (Non-Storable / Storable) — expected, not actionable
Session Management Response Identified — informational only

CSP Notices: ZAP's CSP parser strips the per-request nonce value and reports 'nonce-' (empty), then flags it as an unrecognised nonce. The before_request hook always generates a valid nonce; the report is a scanner artifact.
Dangerous JS Functions — false positive in htmx.min.js (vendor library); eval-like patterns are internal HTMX implementation details, not application code

Authentication Request Identified — informational notice that ZAP recognized /login as an auth endpoint (the 400 is Flask-WTF correctly rejecting a CSRF-less/malformed scanner probe); not a vulnerability

column1: # False positives confirmed after first ZAP full (active) scan run (2026-07-22)

column3: Cookie Slack Detector: all flagged instances (/, /demo, /login, /set-language) are intentionally public, unauthenticated routes — dropping a cookie doesn't change their response because none of them gate on one; the full-scan crawl never authenticates, so it never reaches a page where this check would be meaningful

User Agent Fuzzer: informational-only self-documentation of the check ZAP ran (tries a few old-IE User-Agent strings); no evidence of an actual behavioural difference was reported, and ZAP's own risk gate already treats it as PASS, not WARN/FAIL
